data.title: Fundstrat’s Tom Lee: bullish on AI’s long-term growth potential
data.description:
Fundstrat Global Advisors’ Tom Lee maintained his bullish market stance despite widespread criticism, pointing to artificial intelligence as a major driver for continued growth.

Lee emphasized that AI adoption is still in its early stages, comparing it to the wireless industry’s decades-long expansion from 37 million to 7 billion subscribers. “We’re probably in the first quarter or first third of that cycle for AI,” the strategist said in an interview with CNBC.

When markets declined between February and April, Lee’s team evaluated whether economic fundamentals warranted panic. “We took a step back and said, with all the forces at work, are we really going to drive the economy off a cliff?” he explained, concluding that holding positions or buying during periods of fear was the appropriate strategy. He believes the S&P 500 will reach 6,800-7,000 by the end of the year.

He also drew parallels between current AI infrastructure investments and historical technology build-outs like Global Crossing’s (LUMN) oceanic cable installations. According to Lee, the current phase focuses on building foundational elements like large language models and data centers, similar to how wireless infrastructure preceded application development. This pattern suggests that while initial investors may face challenges, subsequent opportunities often emerge as the technology matures.

Lee addressed concerns about companies spending heavily on AI without immediate returns, explaining that this mirrors typical technology adoption cycles. He noted that in cases like Global Crossing, “the second owner of the hotel made a lot of money,” suggesting that value often accrues to later participants after initial infrastructure investments.

The current focus on AI security and validation systems represents the next wave of development before widespread business implementation, he said.

He also expressed confidence that companies like JPMorgan (JPM) and Goldman Sachs (GS) will soon leverage AI to enhance their business strategies.

“There are still a lot of positives to come,” Lee concluded, reinforcing his view that the AI revolution still has substantial growth potential despite short-term market fluctuations and investment concerns.

data.title: Venture Global raised to Buy at Deutsche Bank after Plaquemines LNG site tour
data.description:
Venture Global (NYSE:VG) -1.3% in Thursday's trading despite winning an upgrade to Buy from Hold with a $17 price target at Deutsche Bank, saying a visit to the Plaquemines LNG plant with company management including CEO Mike Sabel provided "a much better understanding of just how complex, disciplined, and deliberate the sequencing of construction activities are to allow for the simultaneous commissioning and construction work that occurs at these projects."

Deutsche Bank analyst Chris Robertson said he left the visit with a deeper appreciation for "the scope of modularization and standardization" at the facility, which is "much broader than just the mid-scale liquefaction trains and includes the pre-treatment units and power modules."

While Venture Global (NYSE:VG) is best known for its approach in using factory-built, mid-scale liquefaction trains, "we think that this barely scratches the surface of how the company is differentiating its simultaneous commissioning and construction process."

data.title: EU proposes scrapping tariffs on U.S. industrial goods; seeks lower auto duties
data.description:
The European Union is preparing to remove all tariffs on U.S. industrial imports and widen access for American agricultural products as part of a broader trade deal aimed at protecting European automakers from steep U.S. levies.

The plan follows last week’s announcement that Brussels and Washington had struck an agreement to scale back U.S. tariffs on European autos to 15%, down from a provisional rate of 27.5%. In exchange, the EU pledged to reduce trade barriers for a variety of American goods.

According to a statement released Thursday by the European Commission, these measures mark the first stage of the agreement and are intended to guarantee that tariff relief for European carmakers takes effect retroactively from August 1. EU Trade Commissioner Maros Sefcovic said U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick assured European officials that the reduced rate would apply from the start of the month.

The deal comes as European auto manufacturers face mounting pressure. Brands ranging from Stellantis, the maker of Jeep, to luxury names like Porsche, have reported sharp drops in U.S. sales following the earlier round of tariff hikes.

Under the new proposal, the EU would abolish tariffs on U.S. industrial goods outright and grant preferential access to select American farm and seafood products. A separate measure would allow tariff-free entry for U.S. lobster, including processed shipments.

The proposals must still be reviewed and approved by other EU institutions. The European Commission said the moves are designed to bring “greater stability and predictability” to transatlantic trade and investment.

data.title: S&P 500 hits 6,500 points for the first time ever after economic data and Nvidia results
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes are moving higher on Thursday, as investors weigh a wave of economic data and Nvidia’s (NVDA) latest earnings, all as the S&P 500 surpasses the 6,500 mark.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) finds itself near even. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) has climbed 0.3%, and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) moved higher by 0.6%.

From a sector-by-sector vantage point, six of the 11 S&P segments are up in the green with Communication Services and Info Tech leading the way. On the other end, Health Care has suffered the most so far on the day. 

Shifting to the bond market, the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) has moved down 2 basis points to 4.21%, and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) has moved higher by 2 basis points to 3.63%. Also, a $44B 7-year Treasury note auction tailed, indicating weaker demand for government-backed fixed-income securities.

On the economic front, Q2 GDP growth was revised higher to 3.3%, while the PCE increase was revised lower to 2.0%. 

Furthermore, initial jobless claims  unexpectedly fell in the past week. Q2 corporate profits bounced back, rising 2.0%. 

Additionally, pending home sales dipped 0.4% in July, which was weaker than economists had forecasted. 

Lisa Cook filed a lawsuit challenging President Donald Trump's move to oust her from the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, escalating the conflict between the White House and the U.S. central bank.

Nvidia (NVDA) remains in focus as shares dip 0.2% after the firm reported fiscal second-quarter results and guidance that topped Wall Street's expectations and unveiled a massive share buyback program.

As for stocks that are on move, shares of Snowflake (SNOW) have surged 22.6% after the company produced a strong earnings report.

data.title: Green Plains upgraded at Oppenheimer as ethanol plant sale clears debt overhang
data.description:
Green Plains (NASDAQ:GPRE) +11% to a YTD high in Thursday's trading after reaching a deal to sell its Rives, Tennessee, ethanol plant to POET LLC for $190M, in the first significant move by new CEO Chris Osowski after being named to the post last week.

Green Plains (NASDAQ:GPRE) said it will use the proceeds to strengthen liquidity and repay nearly $128M in junior mezzanine notes, against which the company had pledged nearly all its plants as collateral.

The ability to repay the debt marks a "significant milestone in enhancing our financial flexibility and advancing Green Plains' carbon reduction strategy," CEO Chris Osowski said.

In response, Oppenheimer analysts led by Kristen Owen upgraded Green Plains (GPRE) to Outperform from Perform with a $14 price target, saying the company has removed the largest financial overhang on the stock and strengthened its balance sheet, also enabling the company to conclude its 18-month strategic review process.

"After embarking on an ambitious portfolio transformation, a leadership refresh, and debt retirement, we believe Green Plains is exiting its 18-month strategic review on a stronger financial and operational footprint," Oppenheimer said.

data.title: July's PCE inflation report is expected to show gradually increasing pressures for tariffs
data.description:
Core PCE inflation, a gauge the Federal Reserve closely monitors, is expected to be little changed in July, when the Commerce Department’s Bureau of Economic Analysis releases its Personal Income and Outlays data on Friday. But little changed doesn't mean unchanged.

Core PCE, which excludes food and energy prices, is expected to rise 0.3% M/M, according to the consensus of economists, unchanged from the 0.3% increase in June. On a year-over-year basis, though, the metric is seen rising 2.9% vs. 2.8% in the prior month, still above the Fed's 2% goal.

The core number is carefully watched by Fed policymakers, as they consider it a better indicator of the underlying inflation than the headline number (which includes food and energy) or the Consumer Price Index.

The rate of inflation is crucial in the Federal Open Market Committee’s decision-making process for setting interest rates. If inflation is running hotter-than-desired, the FOMC will lean toward raising rates. But if price increases are subsiding, they’ll be more amenable to lowering rates. Labor market conditions are also key in the Fed’s rate decisions.

Economists estimate that the headline PCE number will rise 0.2% M/M vs. 0.3%  in June and 2.6% Y/Y, unchanged from the previous month’s increase. The consensus estimates for headline and core rates generally align with the Cleveland Fed’s Inflation Nowcasting models.

Investors will keep their eyes peeled on categories that are heavily imported, such as furniture and appliances, to assess the impact of Trump’s tariff policy. Tariffs were only starting to seep into the data in June.

Don't expect any big impact from tariffs in the July data, though. For one thing, businesses, unsure of the ultimate level of tariffs they'll have to deal with, have been reluctant to announce price increases only to have the tariffs that justified the increase come down the next day, Comerica Chief Economist Bill Adams said in an interview with Seeking Alpha. In addition, many importers stocked up on inventories earlier in the year, allowing them to delay price increases as they work off the pre-tariff inventory.

"It looks like PCE inflation will pick up slightly from what we had in June," he said. "The trend in recent months has been that inflation has picked up since the spring, as tariff costs have made their way from port, to warehouse to the checkout aisle."

That process still has months to run.  "Comerica is forecasting for tariff pass-through to be a gradual process and will likely peak around the turn of the year," he said. 

Comerica forecasts that the July PCE index will increase 0.2% M/M, in line with consensus, and core PCE to rise 0.4%, slightly higher than the +0.3% consensus.

"The big picture is that price pressures are gradually reflecting the cost of tariffs affecting goods in the economy, which is being partially offset by less inflation pressure from housing," Adams added.

The effects of tariffs will most likely be seen in manufactured goods, such as durable goods including furniture and appliances, and non-durables, including kitchenware, he said. "Tariffs may also show up in some foods prices in the PCE report. There was a big spike in vegetable prices in the PPI report, and often the PCE components track with the PPI report."

Prices for spices, which fueled the growth of world trade hundreds and thousands of years ago, will likely see the clearest effect of tariffs over the next couple of months.

The pass-through of tariffs into goods prices is "more a question of 'when,' rather than 'if,' so it's of secondary importance to the inflation outlook over a multi-quarter time horizon," Adams said. "Probably more important is what’s happening to core services prices, excluding energy and housing, which have slowed into 2025. But we did see a pickup in some services prices in the PPI report."

Housing inflation, which was stubbornly sticky for years after the pandemic, is now easing. "Shelter inflation has slowed in the CPI and PCE indexes over the last year, and it seems quite likely it’ll continue to slow," he added.  "But the question is: how much? And how much does that reduction in shelter inflation offset higher prices from manufactured goods?"

He also expects that Friday's personal income and outlays report, which includes the July PCE data, will be a solid month for consumer spending. Consumers rushed to buy electric vehicles ahead of tax credits phasing out. "And consumer spending on gasoline and summer travel were likely boosts to spending on non-durable goods and services," he said.

Overall, Comerica's Adams sees the economy as holding up well. "The trade policy uncertainty has made 2025 a bumpier year than expected in January, but the economy is continuing to grow," he said.

data.title: Wynn Resorts gains after UBS points to upside from the Al Marjan project
data.description:
UBS boosted its rating on Wynn Resorts (NASDAQ:WYNN) to Buy from Hold after taking a more constructive view on the upside potential from the casino company's Al Marjan resort.

The Al Marjan resort project in Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates, is a $3.9 billion luxury integrated resort featuring 1,542 rooms, 22 private villas, a casino, 22 restaurants, a marina, extensive retail, and convention facilities, set on white sand beaches with a planned opening in 2027.

Analyst Robin Farley and team think the market will come to share the positive view on Al Marjan after Wynn Resorts' (NASDAQ:WYNN) Investor Day event in December.

"We estimate run rate adj property EBITDAM of $730M, representing meaningful upside to WYNN's base case adj property EBITDAM estimate of $625M. We anticipate that WYNN being theonly gaming operator in the UAE should provide a meaningful head start in capturing loyalty among ultra high net worth international customers," previewed Farley.

UBS boosted its price target on Wynn Resorts (WYNN) to $147 from $101.

Shares of Wynn Resorts (WYNN) pushed 1.9% higher in afternoon trading.

data.title: Anthtopic trains models with user chats, extends data retention to five years
data.description:
Anthropic, an artificial intelligence startup backed by tech giants such as Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), utilizes chat data from users on its Claude Free, Pro and Max plans to train new large language models.

The company announced today it is allowing current users until Sept. 28, 2025, to turn this feature off. New users will pick the setting for model training during the signup process.

"This setting will only apply to new or resumed chats and coding sessions on Claude," Anthropic said. "Previous chats with no additional activity will not be used for model training."

Anthropic is also extending its data retention period to five years for all users who agree to allow chat data for training and model improvement. 

"By participating, you'll help us improve model safety, making our systems for detecting harmful content more accurate and less likely to flag harmless conversations," Anthropic said. "You'll also help future Claude models improve at skills like coding, analysis, and reasoning, ultimately leading to better models for all users."

The new policy does not apply to users under Anthropic's commercial terms, including Claude for Work, Claude Gov, Claude for Education, or API use, including via third parties such as Amazon Bedrock and Google Cloud's Vertex AI. 

Anthropic is not the only AI company that uses chat data for training models. Google Gemini also stores conversations to train its machine learning systems. This feature can also be turned on and off. Microsoft-backed (MSFT) OpenAI's ChatGPT also has this feature, which users can turn off. Meta Platforms (META) uses public posts from Facebook and Instagram for training.

data.title: Barclays to sell Entercard JV stake to partner Swedbank
data.description:
Barclays (NYSE:BCS) (OTCPK:BCLYF) is set to sell its joint venture stake in the Nordics-based credit market company Entercard to partner Swedbank (OTCPK:SWDBF) (OTCPK:SWDBY).

Swedbank and Barclays have co-owned Entercard since 2005.

The purchase price will be based on an amount corresponding to 50% of the unit's equity at the time of acquisition. Swedbank is expected to acquire the U.K.-based lender's stake at book value, estimated to be SEK2.6B as of March 31.

The consideration is payable in cash on completion of the transaction, expected to close by the end of 2025.

The acquisition is expected to negatively impact Swedbank's common equity Tier 1 capital ratio by ~30 basis points.

data.title: RFK Jr. to testify before Senate committee as he speaks out on CDC director firing
data.description:
HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said that CDC Director Susan Monarez was fired from that position on Wednesday in part because she wasn't following President Trump's agenda.

"We need to look at the priorities of the agency, if there's really a deeply, deeply embedded I would say malaise at the agency. We need strong leadership that will go in there and be able to execute on President Trump's broad ambitions," the secretary said Thursday morning on Fox News' "Fox & Friends."

RFK Jr. added that while the agency is "in trouble, we're fixing it."

Problems at the CDC RFK Jr. cited were mostly related to its handling of Covid-19. He accused the agency of issuing misinformation, getting testing, social distancing, and masks wrong, and causing harm to children by closing schools. 

Separately, RFK Jr. will testify before the Senate Finance Committee on Sept. 4 to discuss the president's health care agenda. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
